elements.sent-2411-001: Convex quadratically constrained quadratic programs (QCQPs) involve finding a solution within a convex feasible region defined by quadratic constraints while minimizing a convex quadratic objective function.
elements.sent-2411-002: These problems arise in various industrial applications, including power systems and signal processing.
elements.sent-2411-003: Traditional methods for solving convex QCQPs primarily rely on matrix factorization, which quickly becomes computationally prohibitive as the problem size increases.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recently, graph neural networks (GNNs) have gained attention for their potential in representing and solving various optimization problems such as linear programs and linearly constrained quadratic programs.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we are the first to investigate the representation power of GNNs in the context of QCQP tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we propose a new tripartite graph representation for general convex QCQPs and properly associate it with message-passing GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that there exist GNNs capable of reliably representing key properties of convex QCQPs, including feasibility, optimal value, and optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our result deepens the understanding of the connection between QCQPs and GNNs, paving the way for future machine learning approaches to efficiently solve QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-009: Quadratic programs~(QPs) are a pivotal class of optimization problems where the objective function is quadratic, and the constraints are typically linear or quadratic.
elements.sent-2411-010: Based on the nature of constraints, QPs can be further classified as linearly constrained quadratic programs~(LCQPs) and quadratically constrained quadratic programs~(QCQPs).
elements.sent-2411-011: When the objective and constraint matrices are positive semi-definite, the problem becomes a convex QCQP, making it both theoretically interesting and practically important.
elements.sent-2411-012: Convex QCQPs arise in various critical applications such as robust optimization in uncertain environments~\citep{ben2001lectures,boyd2004convex , power flow~\citep{bienstock2020mathematical , and signal processing~\citep{luo2010semidefinite , while ensuring optimality and computational efficiency is paramount.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Solving QPs, especially those with quadratic constraints, presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-014: Traditional methods often involve computationally intensive procedures that would struggle with scalability and real-time processing requirements.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, the interior-point method~\citep{nocedal1999numerical for a general $n$-variable QP involves solving a sequence of linear systems of equations, necessitating matrix decomposition with a runtime complexity of~$\mathbb{R ^s(n^3)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This leads to substantial computational burden in the large-scale case.
elements.sent-2411-017: Similarly, active-set algorithms~\citep{gill2019practical , which work by iteratively adjusting the set of active constraints, can also become computationally demanding as the number of constraints and variables increase.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In recent years, advances in machine learning (ML) have opened new avenues for enhancing the solving process of QPs.
elements.sent-2411-019: There are mainly two categories of ML-aided QP methods.
elements.sent-2411-020: The first category aims to learn adaptive configurations of a specific QP algorithm or solver to accelerate the solving process~\citep{bonami2018learning,ichnowski2021accelerating,jung2022learning , while the second focuses on predicting an initial solution of QPs, which is either directly taken as a final solution or further refined by subsequent algorithms or QP solvers~\citep{bertsimas2022online,gao2021deep,sambharya2023end,tan2024ensemble,wang2020learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Most of these methods utilize graph neural networks~(GNNs) to leverage the structural properties of graph-structured data, making them particularly well-suited for representing the relationships and dependencies inherent in QPs.
elements.sent-2411-022: By encoding QP instances into graphs, GNNs can capture intricate features and provide adaptive guidance or approximate solutions efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-023: In addition to these empirical studies, theoretical research on the expressive power of GNNs \citep{zhang2023expressive, li2022expressive and their relation to optimization problems has further strengthened the understanding of their capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-024: For instance, \cite{chen2022LP and \cite{chen2022MILP established theoretical foundations for applying GNNs to solving linear programs~(LPs) and mixed-integer linear programs, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Further, such foundations are extended to LCQPs and their discrete variant, mixed-integer LCQPs in \cite{chen2024expressive .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Previous studies have empirically and theoretically demonstrated the utility of GNNs in speeding up existing QP solvers and directly approximating solutions for various QP instances.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, there is a noticeable lack of research on their use in QCQPs, particularly in how they handle quadratic constraints.
elements.sent-2411-028: Existing graph representations used for LCQPs~\citep{chen2024expressive are inadequate for QCQPs as they can not capture the complex interactions introduced by quadratic constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, the question of whether GNNs can accurately predict key properties of QCQPs, such as feasibility, optimal objective value, and optimal solution, remains open.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper aims to address the aforementioned gap by exploring both theoretical foundations and practical implementation of using GNNs for solving convex QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, we propose a tripartite graph representation for general convex QCQPs, and establish theoretical foundations of applying GNNs to optimize QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-032: The distinct contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose a novel tripartite graph representation for general QCQPs, which divides a QCQP into three types of nodes: linear-term, quadratic-term, and constraint nodes, with edges added between heterogeneous nodes to indicate problem parameters.
elements.sent-2411-034: This representation effectively addresses the limitations of existing graph representations for LCQPs, i.e., those graphs are unable to capture the interactions imposed by quadratic constraints.
elements.sent-2411-035: We conduct analysis on the separation power as well as approximation power of message-passing GNNs~(MP-GNNs).
elements.sent-2411-036: We showed that MP-GNNs are capable of capturing some key properties of convex QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-037: We conduct initial numerical tests of the tripartite message-passing GNNs on small QCQP instances.
elements.sent-2411-038: The results showed that MP-GNNs can be trained to approximate the key properties well.
elements.sent-2411-039: Throughout this paper, scalars or vectors are denoted by lowercase letters (e.g., $a$), and matrices are denoted by uppercase letters (e.g., $A$).
elements.sent-2411-040: For a vector $a$, we denote its $i$-th entry by $a_i$.
elements.sent-2411-041: For a matrix $A$, the entry in the $i$-th row and the $j$-th column is denoted by $a_{i,j}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We use $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$ to denote vectors or matrices with all-zero and all-one entries, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-043: For any positive integers $m,n$ with $m<n$, we define $[m,n] \coloneqq \{m, m+1,\cdots, n\}$ to be the set of all integers ranging from $m$ to $n$.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this work, we study QCQPs defined in the following form: \begin{equation}\label{eq:QCQP} \begin{aligned} \min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \frac{1}{2}x^\top Q x + p^\top x \;\; \mathrm{s.t.} \frac{1}{2} x^\top Q^i x + (p^i)^\top x + b^i \leq 0 \forall ~i\in [m] x^{\text{L}} \leq x \leq x^{\text{U}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $Q, Q^i \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$, $p,p^i \in \mathbb{R ^{n}$, $b^i\in \mathbb{R}$, $x^{\text{L \in (\mathbb{R \cup \{-\infty\})^n$, and $x^{\text{U \in (\mathbb{R \cup \{+\infty\})^n$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:QCQP}  \min_{x \in R^n} \frac{1}{2}x^\top Q x + p^\top x \;\; s.t. \frac{1}{2} x^\top Q^i x + (p^i)^\top x + b^i \leq 0 \forall ~i\in [m] x^{L} \leq x \leq x^{U} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: The problem has $n$ optimization variables and $m$ constraints.
elements.sent-2411-047: We refer to the tuple $(m,n)$ as the problem size of QCQP.
elements.sent-2411-048: Both the objective function and the constraints are associated with quadratic functions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Without loss of generality, we assume $Q$ and $Q^i$'s are all symmetric matrices.
elements.sent-2411-050: The QCQP problem is convex if $Q$ and $Q^i$'s are all positive semidefinite.
elements.sent-2411-051: We denote the feasible set of Problem \ref{eq:QCQP by \begin{equation}\mathcal{X} \coloneqq \!\! \left\{x\in \mathbb{R}^n:\quad \frac{1}{2} x^\top Q^i x + (p^i)^{\top} x + b^i \leq 0, \quad \!\! \forall i \in [m], \quad\!\! x^{\text{L}} \leq x \leq x^{\text{U}}\right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}X \coloneqq \!\! \left\{x\in R^n:\quad \frac{1}{2} x^\top Q^i x + (p^i)^{\top} x + b^i \leq 0, \quad \!\! \forall i \in [m], \quad\!\! x^{L} \leq x \leq x^{U}\right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: If $\mathcal{X \neq \emptyset$, the QCQP is said to be feasible; otherwise, it is said to be infeasible.}
elements.sent-2411-053: A feasible QCQP is said to be bounded if the objective is bounded from below on $\mathcal{X}$, i,e., there exists $z \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $\frac{1 {2 x^\top Q x + p^\top x \geq z$ for every $x \in \mathcal{X}$; otherwise, it is said to be unbounded.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For a feasible and bounded QCQP, $x^* \in \mathcal{X}$ is said to be an optimal solution if \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}x^{*\top} Q x^* + p^\top x^* \leq \frac{1}{2}x^{\top} Q x + p^\top x\end{equation} for every $x \in \mathcal{X}$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}x^{*\top} Q x^* + p^\top x^* \leq \frac{1}{2}x^{\top} Q x + p^\top x\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: We remark that a QCQP always admits an optimal solution if it is feasible and bounded, but such an optimal solution might not be unique.
elements.sent-2411-056: The first theoretical result demonstrating the representation power of GNNs in solving optimization problems was provided by Yin et al. \cite{chen2022LP .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this work, the information of an LP problem is encoded into a bipartite graph, where each of the variables and constraints are modeled as nodes, and their association is captured as edges.
elements.sent-2411-058: They showed that GNNs utilizing this graph representation can universally approximate the optimal solution of LPs, as well as properties of feasibility and boundness.
elements.sent-2411-059: This bipartite graph modeling was later extended to analyze the representation power of GNNs for LCQPs \cite{chen2024expressive .}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, extending the traditional graph modeling to QCQP poses significant challenges due to the presence of quadratic constraints, and it remains unclear what type of graph is suitable for representing QCQPs in the context of GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-061: To fill this gap, we introduce a tripartite graph representation $G_{\mathrm{QCQP \coloneqq (V, E)$ that encodes all elements of a QCQP~(\ref{eq:QCQP ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Compared to the traditional bipartite graph modeling for LPs and LCQPs, our tripartite graph representation introduces an additional class of nodes to model the quadratic terms of variables.
elements.sent-2411-063: This modification allows us to represent QCQPs without any loss of information.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this paper, we will show that the tripartite representation enables GNNs to universally approximate solutions for convex QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-065: Formally, a tripartite graph modeling a QCQP consists of three types of nodes, representing variables, constraints, and quadratic terms respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: Specifically, we define $V_1 \coloneqq \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n\}$ as the set of nodes where each $u_i$ corresponds to the variable $x_i$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Each node $u_i$ is associated with a feature tuple $(p_i, x^{\text{L _i, x^{\text{U _i)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Next, we define $V_2 \coloneqq \{v_{j,k : (j, k) \in \mathcal{L}\}$ as the set of nodes representing the quadratic terms, where $\mathcal{L \coloneqq \{(j, k) \in [n] \times [n]: j\leq k, |q_{j,k | + \sum_{i \in [m] |q^i_{j,k | > 0\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: We remark that if $(j,k) \in L$, the coefficient of the quadratic term $x_jx_k$ is non-zero in the objective function or at least one of the constraints.
elements.sent-2411-070: For each node in $V_2$, if $j>k$, $v_{i,j}$ is associated with a feature $2q_{j,k}$; if $j=k$, $v_{j,j}$ is associated with a feature $q_{j,j}$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Further, we define $V_3 \coloneqq \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}$ as the set of nodes where each $c_i$ represents the $i$-th constraint, with each node $c_i$ associated with a feature $b_i$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, the set of all nodes in the QCQP graph is given by $V \coloneqq V_1 \cup V_2 \cup V_3$.\footnote{We always denote a variable node by $u$, a quadratic node by $v$, and a constraint node $c$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: We always index the constraint nodes by $i$, and the variable/quadratic nodes by $j,k$, unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-074: The QCQP graph also includes three types of edges.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $E_{12 \coloneqq \left\{(u_{j' , v_{j,k ) \in V_1 \times V_2 : j' = j \lor j' = k \right\}$ be the set of edges connecting nodes from $V_1$ to those in $V_2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The weight of an edge $(u_{j' , v_{j,k )$ is $1$ if $j > k$ and $2$ otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Let $E_{13 \coloneqq \{(u_j, c_i) \in V_1 \times V_3 : p^i_j \neq 0 \}$ be the set of edges connecting nodes from $V_1$ to those in $V_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The weight of an edge $(u_j, c_i)$ is $p^i_j$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let $E_{23 \coloneqq \{(v_{j,k , c_i) \in V_2 \times V_3 : q^i_{j,k \neq 0 \}$ be the set of edges connecting nodes from $V_2$ to those in $V_3$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The weight of an edge $(v_{j,k , c_i)$ is $2q^i_{j,k}$ if $j > k$ and $q^i_{j,j}$ otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Thus, the set of all edges is given by $E \coloneqq E_{12 \cup E_{13 \cup E_{23}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Throughout this paper, we denote the weight of the edge between $u\in V_1$ and $v\in V_2$ by $w_{u,v =w_{v,u}$, and similarly $w_{u,c =w_{c,u}$ for edges between $V_1,V_3$, $w_{v,c =w_{c,v}$ for edges between $V_2,V_3$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-088: To study the capability of GNNs in representing QCQPs, we tailor the general message-passing GNNs for the tripartite nature of the introduced QCQP graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-089: An overview is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:GNN .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Specifically, we consider the family of tripartite message-passing GNNs consisting of an embedding layer, $T$ message-passing layers (each comprised of four sub-layers), and a readout layer, detailed as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-091: For all nodes, the input features $h^{0,u ,h^{0,v ,h^{0,c}$ are initialized by embedding the node features into a hidden space $\mathbb{R ^{h_0}$, where $h_0$ is the space dimension.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, \begin{equation}h^{0,u} \gets g^0_1(h^u), \forall u\in V_1 \quad h^{0,v} \gets g^0_2(h^v), \forall v\in V_2, \quad h^{0,c} \gets g^0_3(h^c), \forall c\in V_3\end{equation} where $g^0_l$'s are learnable embedding functions, $l=1,2,3$, and $h^u,h^v,h^c$ are the node features carried by $u\in V_1,v\in V_2,c\in V_3$.

elements.sent-2411-106: We define the following target functions, characterizing some key properties on learning an end-to-end network to predict the optimal solutions of convex QCQPs:.
elements.sent-2411-107: We define the following target mappings.
elements.sent-2411-108: Feasibility mapping: We define $\Phi_{\mathrm{feas (G_{\mathrm{QCQP ) = 1$ if the QCQP problem is feasible and $\Phi_{\mathrm{feas (G_{\mathrm{QCQP ) = 0$ otherwise.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Boundedness mapping: for a feasible QCQP problem, we define $\Phi_{\mathrm{bound (G_{\mathrm{QCQP ) = 1$ if the QCQP problem is bounded and $\Phi_{\mathrm{bound (G_{\mathrm{QCQP ) = 0$ otherwise.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Optimal value mapping: for a feasible and bounded QCQP problem, we set $\Phi_{\mathrm{opt (G_{\mathrm{QCQP )$ to be its optimal objective value.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Optimal solution mapping: for a feasible, bounded QCQP problem, there must exist at least an optimal solution, but the optimal solution might not be unique.
elements.sent-2411-112: However, if the QCQP is convex, there exists a unique optimal solution $x^*$ with the smallest $\ell_2$-norm among all optimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-113: Therefore, for convex QCQP we define the optimal solution mapping to be $\Phi_{\mathrm{sol (G_{\mathrm{QCQP )$ = $x^*$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Since the optimal solution with the smallest $\ell_2$-norm may not be unique for non-convex QCQP, we do not define its optimal solution mapping.\footnote{In fact, Section \ref{sec:non_convex_counter_examples shows that there exists a pair of non-convex QCQPs that cannot be distinguished by any GNNs.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Thus, even if an optimal solution mapping for non-convex QCQPs is defined, GNNs cannot universally approximate it.
elements.sent-2411-116: Now, we demonstrate that for convex QCQPs, any target function in Definition \ref{def:QCQP_properties can be universally approximated by message-passing GNNs.}

elements.sent-2411-118: For any probability measure $\mathbb{P}$ on the space of convex QCQPs $\mathcal{G ^{m,n _{\mathrm{QCQP }$ and any $\delta, \varepsilon > 0$, there exists $F \in \mathcal{F ^{m,n _{\mathrm{QCQP (\mathbb{R ^s)$ such that for any target mapping $\Phi:\mathcal{G ^{m,n _{\mathrm{QCQP }\to\mathbb{R ^s$ defined in Definition~\ref{def:QCQP_properties , we have \begin{equation}\mathbb{P}\left\{||F(G_{\mathrm{QCQP}})-\Phi(G_{\mathrm{QCQP}})|| > \delta \right\} < \varepsilon. \label{eq:main_theorem}\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Message-passing GNNs can not represent non-convex QCQPs
elements.sent-2411-121: In contrast to convex QCQPs, message-passing GNNs based on tripartite graph representation do not possess universal representation power for non-convex QCQPs.
elements.sent-2411-122: Formally, we have the following propositions.
elements.sent-2411-123: There exists non-convex QCQP instances $\mathcal{I ,\bar{\mathcal{I }$ encoded by tripartite graph representation $G,\Bar{G}$ respectively, such that $\Phi(G)_{\text{feas }\not=\Phi_{\text{feas (\Bar{G )$, but any GNN $F\in\mathcal{F _{\mathrm{QCQP (\mathbb{R )$ gives $F(G)=F(\Bar{G )$.}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-164: This paper introduces a new tripartite graph representation specifically designed for QCQPs.

elements.sent-2411-167: This research contributes to the field of learning to optimize (L2O) by expanding the application of GNNs to QCQP problems, which were previously challenging for traditional graph-based L2O methods.

elements.sent-2411-381: In this work, we investigate the representation power of GNNs in the context of QCQP tasks.
elements.sent-2411-382: Additionally, \cite{xiong2024neuralqp propose a hypergraph-based framework aligning the interior-point method for solving QCQPs.}
elements.sent-2411-383: In addition to these studies, theoretical research on the expressive power of GNNs \citep{zhang2023expressive, li2022expressive and their relation to optimization problems has further strengthened the understanding of their capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-384: However, the question of whether GNNs can accurately predict key properties of QCQPs, such as feasibility, optimal objective value, and optimal solution, remains open.
elements.sent-2411-385: In this work, the information of an LP problem is encoded into a bipartite graph, where variables and constraints are modeled as nodes, and their association is represented as edges.
elements.sent-2411-386: They showed that GNNs based on this graph representation can universally approximate the optimal solution of LPs, as well as properties of feasibility and boundedness.
elements.sent-2411-387: This bipartite graph modeling was later extended to analyze the representation power of GNNs for LCQPs~\citep{chen2024expressive .}
elements.sent-2411-388: Despite these advances, it remains challenging to develop graph representation to encode all information of general QCQPs while maintaining simplicity for GNN processing.
elements.sent-2411-389: Due to the presence of quadratic terms, a QCQP generally involves $O(n^2 \times m)$ coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-390: Consequently, a graph encoding all QCQP information inherently exhibits a complexity of the same order, $O(n^2 \times m)$.
elements.sent-2411-391: There are two natural extensions of the traditional bipartite representation of LP/LCQP to QCQP.
elements.sent-2411-392: This approach adds hyperedges to the traditional bipartite graph to represent quadratic coefficients, turning the graph into a hypergraph.
elements.sent-2411-393: However, to the best of our knowledge, current GNN architectures struggle to handle hyperedges efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-395: In this method, all coefficients are encoded as features associated with the $n$ variable nodes and the $m$ constraint nodes, resulting in a graph with vector features of varying sizes, depending on the problem.
elements.sent-2411-396: However, existing GNNs are generally incapable of processing features of varying dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-397: To fill this gap, we introduce an undirected tripartite graph representation $G_{\mathrm{QCQP \coloneqq (V, E)$ that encodes all elements of a QCQP~(\ref{eq:QCQP ).}}}
